data.title: Mid-America Apartment raised to Outperform at Scotiabank as FFO outlook brightens
data.description:
Scotiabank upgraded Mid-America Apartment Communities (NYSE:MAA) to Sector Outperform from Sector Perform, citing an improved outlook for FFO-per-share growth starting in 2026.

Based on Scotia's analysis of RealPage data, "we see light at the end of the tunnel for new supply impact in Sunbelt markets," analyst Nicholas Yulico wrote in a note to clients. He pointed to RealPage data showing that U.S. multifamily effective rents rose 0.6% Y/Y in January, up from +0.5% in December.

"We forecast Sunbelt rent growth to improve in H2/25, combined with MAA's growing development NOI contribution," he said. Scotiabank models FFO-per-share growth improving from -0.8% in 2025 (vs. multifamily REIT peers at +1.8%) to +5.2% in 2026 (vs. MF REIT peers at +4.8%).

Yulico's Outperform rating on Mid-America Apartment (NYSE:MAA) contrasts with the SA Quant rating and average SA Analyst rating, both at Hold, and aligns with the average Wall Street rating of Buy.

Mid-America Apartment (MAA) stock rose 0.4% in midday Friday trading.

data.title: Renaissance Technologies top Q4 moves include taking in Intel, exiting Arm & upping Nike
data.description:
Renaissance Technologies took a new stake in Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), exited its position in Arm (NASDAQ:ARM), significantly upped its holdings in Nike (NYSE:NKE) and almost entirely sold out of Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) in Q4 2024, among other moves.

Renaissance disclosed the information in its latest 13F filing for the three months ended December 31, 2024, published on Thursday.

The fund picked up 4.66M shares in Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), even as the legacy chipmaker's stock sees a resurgence after a troubled 2024. The fund also took new stakes of 278.7K shares in managed care behemoth UnitedHealth (UNH) and 921.5K shares in pharmaceutical major Johnson & Johnson (JNJ). Additionally, it took in 637.1K class A shares of home builder Lennar (LEN).

Turning to exits, Renaissance pulled out of British chip designer Arm (NASDAQ:ARM), health insurer Humana (HUM), corporate services provider Cintas (CTAS) and index provider MSCI (MSCI).

Furthermore, the hedge fund exited its entire position in Alphabet's class C stock (NASDAQ:GOOG) and trimmed its holdings in the Google-parent's class A stock (NASDAQ:GOOGL) by 99.6% to 3K. In another Magnificent 7 move, it slashed its stake in Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) by 99.8% to 2.4K shares.

Other reductions include a 92.3% decrease in class A shares of Facebook-parent Meta Platforms (META) to about 50K, a 92.1% trim in memory chipmaker Micron Technology (MU) to around 205K shares, and a 79% cut in the world's largest pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly (LLY) to 54.1K shares.

In terms of stake increases, the hedge fund massively upped its class B Nike (NYSE:NKE) shares to 2.60M in Q4 from 185.5K in Q3.

It also nearly quadrupled its holdings in social media company Reddit (RDDT) to 1.65M class A shares, upped its position in the world's largest contract chipmaker Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) by 92% to 1.63M American depositary shares, and raised its stake in the world's biggest music streaming firm Spotify (SPOT) by 49.5% to 1.02M shares.

Founded by mathematician Jim Simons in 1982, Renaissance Technologies is one of the largest hedge funds in the U.S.

More on 13F filings

  • Soros Capital takes new Nvidia, Chipotle stakes, exits Alibaba, Microsoft, among top Q4 moves
  • SoundHound AI, Serve, Nano-X in focus as Nvidia exits stakes; cuts Arm investment
  • Ray Dalio's Bridgewater takes new stake in Tesla, exits Micron & Goldman Sachs in Q4 moves
  • Seth Klarman's Baupost piles into Restaurant Brands stake, takes in Humana among Q4 moves
  • David Tepper's Appaloosa beefs up China bets, adds Corning, exits Adobe: top Q4 trades

data.title: Kinsale Capital projects 10%-20% growth with expanded product lines and rising investment returns
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kinsale Capital Group (NYSE:KNSL) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Michael Kehoe highlighted a 19.4% increase in operating earnings per share and a 12.2% growth in gross written premiums compared to the same quarter last year. The company achieved a combined ratio of 73.4% and a full-year operating return on equity of 29%. He attributed these results to Kinsale’s focus on small E&S accounts, technology-driven cost efficiencies, and disciplined underwriting practices.

  • CFO Bryan Petrucelli reported a net investment income increase of 37.8% year-over-year, driven by strong operating cash flows and higher interest rates. The company plans to gradually increase its allocation to common stock from 8% to 12% of cash and invested assets over the next year. He also noted that diluted operating earnings per share for the quarter were $4.62, compared to $3.87 in the prior-year quarter.

  • President and COO Brian Haney highlighted the continued growth in gross written premiums, particularly within casualty underwriting divisions, which grew at 15% for the quarter. He emphasized Kinsale’s ongoing product line expansions, including the launch of a new agribusiness underwriting unit focused on farm and ranch exposures. New business submissions grew 17% in the quarter, down from 23% in Q3, with the overall submission growth viewed as a positive indicator for future performance.

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its long-term growth target of 10%-20% for gross written premiums. CEO Michael Kehoe stated that the company’s competitive advantages, including its low-cost structure and technological capabilities, provide confidence in achieving this target.

  • CFO Bryan Petrucelli expects continued benefits from higher new money yields, averaging in the low 5% range, which should further enhance net investment income moving forward.

  • The company anticipates gradual growth in its personal lines business, with high-value homeowners’ insurance and other catastrophe-exposed property lines identified as key growth areas.

Financial Results

  • Operating earnings per share reached $4.62 for the quarter, compared to $3.87 in Q4 2023. Gross written premiums increased by 12.2% year-over-year.

  • The combined ratio improved to 73.4%, reflecting strong underwriting performance despite $8 million in catastrophe losses from Hurricane Milton and a $25 million pretax loss estimate from the Southern California wildfires.

  • The expense ratio for the full year was 20.6%, slightly lower than 20.8% in 2023, attributed to ceding commissions and disciplined expense management.

  • Net investment income rose by 37.8%, with book yields at 4.5% and new money yields in the low 5% range. The annualized gross return for the year was 4.4%, up from 4% in the prior year.

Q&A

  • Michael Zaremski, BMO Capital Markets, asked about pricing and growth in small commercial casualty lines. CEO Michael Kehoe noted that while larger property accounts faced competitive pressures, small property divisions continue to show rapid growth with positive rate increases.

  • Bill Carcache, Wolfe Research, inquired about the sustainability of Kinsale’s growth rate. CEO Michael Kehoe emphasized that the 10%-20% growth target is achievable due to the company’s durable competitive advantages and strategic investments in technology.

  • Andrew Andersen, Jefferies, questioned the California wildfire losses. CEO Michael Kehoe provided a breakdown, noting a $45 million gross loss, reduced to $25 million pretax through reinsurance.

  • Mark Hughes, Truist Securities, asked about the expense ratio trend. CFO Bryan Petrucelli stated that the quarterly fluctuations are primarily due to variable compensation but emphasized the importance of using the annualized 20.6% as a benchmark.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed skepticism regarding the impact of competitive pressures on growth, particularly in property and casualty lines. Questions focused on pricing strategies and potential market share erosion.

  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing Kinsale’s competitive advantages and long-term growth prospects. CEO Michael Kehoe highlighted the company’s ability to balance profitability and growth, stating, “We are very confident in what we’re doing.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts appeared more cautious about the competitive environment, while management’s tone remained consistent, emphasizing optimism and confidence in strategic initiatives.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Gross written premium growth decelerated to 12.2% in Q4 from 19% in Q3, reflecting increased competition, particularly in larger property accounts.

  • The combined ratio improved to 73.4% in Q4 from 75.7% in Q3, driven by strong underwriting performance and higher investment income.

  • Management maintained its growth guidance of 10%-20%, consistent with Q3, but noted continued competitive pressures in specific lines.

  • Analysts in Q4 focused more on competitive dynamics and potential impacts on growth, whereas Q3 discussions centered on the company’s ability to sustain elevated growth rates.

Risks and Concerns

  • Increased competition in larger property accounts and certain professional liability lines could pressure growth rates.

  • Catastrophe losses from the Southern California wildfires and Hurricane Milton highlight exposure to volatility in catastrophe-prone regions.

  • Analysts raised concerns about potential reserve deficiencies in the broader industry, but management emphasized Kinsale’s conservative reserving practices.

Final Takeaway

Kinsale Capital Group delivered strong Q4 results, supported by disciplined underwriting and strategic investments in technology and new product lines. Management reiterated its confidence in achieving 10%-20% long-term growth, citing competitive advantages and robust operating returns. While competitive pressures in certain lines remain a challenge, the company’s low-cost structure and focus on high-margin opportunities position it for sustained profitability and market share gains.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Leggett & Platt sees 2025 adjusted EPS of $1.00-$1.20 amid restructuring-driven efficiencies
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Leggett & Platt (NYSE:LEG) Q4 2024

Management View

  • CEO Karl Glassman praised the execution of the company’s restructuring plan, which yielded $22 million in EBIT benefit in 2024, surpassing expectations. The plan included the closure of 14 bedding locations and further optimization of hydraulic cylinders and administrative costs.
  • Glassman announced the promotion of Sam Smith to President of the Specialized Products segment, emphasizing his role in advancing operational efficiency improvements within the division.
  • CFO Ben Burns highlighted Q4 2024 revenue of $1.1 billion, a 5% decline year-over-year, impacted by weak demand in residential and automotive markets. Adjusted EPS for the quarter was $0.21.
  • Management noted strong product innovation, including the adoption of semi-finished bedding products like ComfortCore and Combi-Core, as well as partnerships with mattress OEMs for product refreshes.

Outlook

  • The company projects 2025 adjusted EPS in the range of $1.00 to $1.20, reflecting restructuring benefits and operational efficiencies.
  • Full-year sales are anticipated to range from $4.0 billion to $4.3 billion, representing a 2%-9% decline year-over-year, with volume reductions across all segments.
  • Management expects restructuring costs of $30-$40 million in 2025, with total costs reaching $80-$90 million by year-end. Incremental EBIT benefit from restructuring is forecasted at $35-$40 million for the year.
  • Cash from operations is expected to range from $275 million to $325 million in 2025, with the first quarter representing a seasonal low point.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted EBIT for Q4 2024 was $56 million, down $10 million compared to the prior year, due to lower volume and compressed metal margins, partially offset by restructuring benefits and operational efficiency improvements.
  • Full-year adjusted EPS was $1.05, a 24% decline year-over-year, reflecting weaker demand in residential and automotive markets. Total 2024 sales reached $4.4 billion, a 7% decrease from 2023.
  • The company reduced total debt by $126 million in 2024, ending the year at $1.9 billion with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.76x. Liquidity at year-end stood at $793 million.

Q&A

  • Susan Maklari, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the dynamics in the bedding market and the impact of imports. CEO Glassman explained that low-end imports accounted for nearly 50% of units sold, creating challenges for mid-tier and higher-tier price points.
  • Bobby Griffin, Raymond James: Inquired about the bedding segment’s restructuring and its impact on margins. Tyson Hagale described the consolidation of innerspring facilities into four larger plants, optimizing geographic efficiency and manufacturing output.
  • Keith Hughes, Truist Securities: Sought clarification on adjustable bed performance. Glassman attributed weaker revenue to product mix shifts favoring lower-priced models.
  • Peter Keith, Piper Sandler: Asked about the potential impact of tariffs on steel. Glassman noted potential margin benefits at the steel mill but warned of offsetting downstream competitiveness challenges.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic about the restructuring progress but expressed concerns over demand softness and macroeconomic headwinds.
  • Management maintained a measured tone, emphasizing operational improvements and restructuring benefits while acknowledging challenges such as inflation and consumer affordability.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone reflected heightened focus on structural improvements and cautious optimism regarding long-term profitability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management increased the projected annualized EBIT benefit from restructuring to $60-$70 million, up from $50-$60 million in Q3.
  • Full-year 2025 guidance reflects continued caution, with anticipated declines across all segments. This marks a more conservative outlook compared to Q3.
  • Analysts shifted focus in Q4 to specific impacts of restructuring and import dynamics, contrasting with broader market concerns highlighted in Q3.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified macroeconomic risks, including low housing turnover, inflation, and weakened consumer confidence, as key challenges.
  • Analysts raised concerns about ongoing import pressure in the bedding market and automotive headwinds driven by EV program delays and trade-downs.
  • The company’s reliance on volume-sensitive businesses was noted as a vulnerability in the current demand environment.

Final Takeaway

Leggett & Platt’s Q4 2024 earnings call highlighted the company’s ongoing restructuring efforts, which are expected to drive significant EBIT benefits in 2025. While facing demand challenges in residential and automotive markets, management remains focused on operational efficiencies, debt reduction, and product innovation to support long-term growth. Investors should monitor how effectively the company navigates macroeconomic headwinds and leverages its streamlined operations to maintain profitability.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Meta is said to be investing heavily in humanoid robots with AI
data.description:
Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is investing heavily in humanoid AI robots and is forming a new team within the Reality Labs division to develop it, Bloomberg reported Friday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The new Reality Labs subdivision will be led by Marc Whitten, former CEO of GM's autonomous vehicle unit Cruise. Sources told Bloomberg that Whitten will be tasked with hiring around 100 engineers this year for the project.

Meta plans to work on the robot hardware, which will focus on household chores for now. Eventually, it hopes to make the underlying AI, sensors, and software for robots that will be made and sold by a range of companies, sources told Bloomberg.

The company is discussing plans with robotics companies, including Unitree Robotics and Figure AI Inc. Down the line, the social media and tech giant plans to compete with Tesla's (NASDAQ:TSLA) Optimus robot, the report said.

“The core technologies we’ve already invested in and built across Reality Labs and AI are complementary to developing the advancements needed for robotics,” CTO Andrew Bosworth reportedly said in a memo.

“We believe that expanding our portfolio to invest in this field will only accrue value to Meta AI and our mixed and augmented reality programs,” Bosworth reportedly said.

The software, sensors, and computing packages that are already being developed for the company's devices are the same technologies that are needed to power humanoids, and Meta will build some of its own hardware, use off-the-shelf components, and work with existing manufacturers as soon as it can, said the people with knowledge of the project.

They highlighted that making prototypes and hardware is key for testing ahead of deploying a platform, even if the company itself doesn’t ultimately release a branded product.

The company is pitching its work as a benchmark for robot development and is aiming to make the Llama LLM a foundation for robotics researchers around the world, sources told Bloomberg. It will also seek to develop tools for robot safety and its power safety.Meta believes humanoids are still a couple of years away from being widely available—and it could be years before the company’s platform is ready to underpin third-party products. But it will become a major focus for Meta and the broader tech industry, the report said.

CEO Mark Zuckerberg said in January the company is planning to invest $60B to $65B to build out the necessary AI infrastructure over the years. Separately, it has more than half a dozen wearable AI devices in the pipeline due for launch this year.

data.title: Nokia's purchase of Infinera set to win EU approval - Reuters
data.description:
Nokia's (NOK) planned $2.3 billion acquisition of Infinera (INFA) is set to be approved by Europe's antitrust regulator.

The European Commission is expected to clear the transaction without conditions, according to a Reuters report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

The deal was approved in the US in September after the HSR waiting period for the acquisition expired. Taiwan's antitrust regulator approved the Infinera (INFA) purchase on Wednesday.

Infinera (NASDAQ:INFN) said late last month that it's sale to Nokia (NOK) was expected to be completed in Q1 and the deal was awaiting approvals from the EU and Taiwan.

Nokia (NOK) agreed in late June to acquire Infinera (INFN) for $2.3B to strengthen its optical networking position, particularly in North America.

data.title: SA Asks: Which energy companies could benefit from higher tariffs?
data.description:
Much has been written, as of late, about how increased U.S. tariffs on gas and oil could negatively impact the energy sector.

But could some energy companies benefit from higher tariffs?

We asked Seeking Alpha analysts Laura Starks and Long Player their thoughts on the topic.

Laura Starks: Within the U.S., were tariffs on Mexican and Canadian crude to be reimposed, it potentially benefits U.S. oil companies of all sizes that produce oil from the U.S. Bakken, Niobrara, Uinta, Permian, Utica and Eagle Ford basins—companies like Occidental Petroleum (NYSE:OXY), Diamondback Energy (NASDAQ:FANG) and ConocoPhillips (NYSE:COP), as well as smaller and private companies.

Multinationals may have mixed results from upstream and downstream. U.S. oilfield services companies such as Halliburton (NYSE:HAL) and Patterson-UTI Energy (NASDAQ:PTEN) would benefit from higher oil prices as companies increased activity.

However, as likely to have as big an effect is President Trump’s request to U.S. producers and OPEC to supply (“oversupply” from the industry's perspective) enough oil to drive the oil price down to $45/bbl from the current $72-$76/bbl.

Long Player: No companies would be helped because, historically, they tariff us more than we tariff them.

As a country, we export unconventional oil and import heavy oil to make money on the difference in prices. If you start a trading war, you destroy those profits and make our balance of payments worse. We would have to borrow money after aggravating our trading partners. Something similar happens when Canada sends us cheap gas, primarily to the Northwestern states, and we then export the Texas surplus to make $1 or $2 MCF on the pricing difference.

The industry faces a severe upheaval, which is why you saw the tariffs get delayed so fast. Mexico and Canada basically announced what they were already doing as agreed months ago, and we still delayed the tariffs. There was not much new. This is about saying basically "either you do what I say or I will hurt myself, and that will affect you". It might happen, but it is very dangerous to us.

The Smoot Hawley Tariff of 1930 was "icing on the cake" for a series of tariffs thought to be a major cause of the Great Depression. Although there are some that debate that, it is still a mainstream conclusion.

  • Top Energy Stocks
  • Top Integrated Gas and Oil Stocks
  • Top Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing Stocks
  • Top Oil and Gas Storage and Transportation Stocks
